evidence: The docstring defines P(n) as 'the largest prime factor of n' and the statement compares P(n+1) with P(n).
The conjecture asserts the set of n with P(n+1) > P(n) 'has density 1/2', formalized via Set.HasDensity.
The problem asks that the factorial equation n! = a_1!a_2!...a_k! with n-1 > a_1 >= ... >= a_k has only finitely many solutions.

Erdos375Prop states that consecutive composites n+1,...,n+k admit distinct primes p_i dividing n+i, which is precisely Grimm's conjecture; the [RST75] reference is titled 'On Grimm's problem relating to factorisation of a block of consecutive integers'.
The variant deduces the prime-gap bound (n+1).nth Prime - n.nth Prime < p_n^(1/2 - c) from Erdos375Prop.
The variant's docstring states 'if Erdos375Prop is true, then Legendre's conjecture is asymptotically true', asserting a prime in (n^2, (n+1)^2) for large n.
The problem asks whether there are infinitely many n such that the central binomial coefficient (2n choose n) is coprime to 105, formalized via Nat.centralBinom.
The problem bounds the sum of 1/p over primes p <= n not dividing the central binomial coefficient (2n choose n).
S(n) is defined as the largest s such that every binomial coefficient (n choose k), 1 <= k < n, is divisible by some prime power p^s.
The statement quantifies over sets A of Schnirelmann density alpha (schnirelmannDensity A) and asks for a density increment via translates A + b.
The problem asks for a set B which is not an additive basis (formalized as ¬ B.IsWeakAddBasis, with Erdos's at-most-k-summands notion) yet always boosts Schnirelmann density.
The problem asks for infinitely many primes p such that the largest prime divisor of the product of p^2+i for 0 <= i <= k equals p, via Nat.maxPrimeFac.
F(n) is defined as the maximum of m + p(m) over composite m < n, where p(m) is the least prime divisor of m (Nat.minFac).
The maximum defining F(n) ranges over composite m < n (m.Composite in the Lean definition), so compositeness is load-bearing in the statement.
